quiz.question: Staff retention had become a serious problem — talented employees were leaving within two years. What is retention?
options: The act of keeping or continuing to hold — keeping something in place
A challenge that required better pay and working conditions to address
A metric that revealed how well an organisation managed its people
An organisational failure with measurable costs in recruitment and training
quiz.answer: The act of keeping or continuing to hold — keeping something in place
quiz.explanation:
Retention is keeping — continuing to hold onto something.
• retention = the continued holding of something; the ability to remember
• From Latin: 'retinere' — to hold back
• 'Staff retention', 'data retention', 'memory retention' — keeping hold of

quiz.question: Her central contention was that the policy had caused more harm than good. What is a contention?
options: A point argued or claimed — a position maintained in dispute
A conclusion drawn from the available evidence on both sides, as the evidence showed
An argument specific enough to be tested against the facts
A position she was prepared to defend against any objection
quiz.answer: A point argued or claimed — a position maintained in dispute
quiz.explanation:
A contention is an argued position — a claim put forward in a dispute.
• contention = a point argued in debate; competition; conflict
• From Latin: 'contendere' — to strive
• 'My contention is that', 'bone of contention' — argued claim

quiz.question: Three members voted against, seven in favour, and two recorded abstentions. What is abstention?
options: The act of refraining from voting — or from any activity
A formal choice not to participate in a decision that affected others
A deliberate withholding of a vote as a statement of position
The option available to those who wished to record neither support nor opposition
quiz.answer: The act of refraining from voting — or from any activity
quiz.explanation:
Abstention is refraining — formally withholding a vote or action.
• abstention = the act of refraining from voting or from an activity
• From Latin: 'abstinere' — to withhold
• 'Record an abstention', 'abstention from alcohol' — refraining

quiz.question: The patient presented with abdominal distention and discomfort. What is distention?
options: Swelling caused by internal pressure — a bloated or expanded state
A clinical sign that prompted further examination
A physical symptom that could indicate several different conditions
An enlargement visible on examination
quiz.answer: Swelling caused by internal pressure — a bloated or expanded state
quiz.explanation:
Distention is swelling from within — expansion caused by internal pressure.
• distention = the state of being distended; swelling
• From Latin: 'distendere' — to stretch
• 'Abdominal distention', 'bladder distention' — expanded by internal pressure

quiz.question: The arts organisation depended on state subvention to remain solvent. What is a subvention?
options: A grant of money from a government or institution — a subsidy
Public funding without which the organisation could not operate
A form of support that required the recipient to meet certain conditions
Financial support from a body with an interest in the activity's continuation
quiz.answer: A grant of money from a government or institution — a subsidy
quiz.explanation:
A subvention is a subsidy — a grant of money from a public or official body.
• subvention = a grant of money, especially from a government
• From Latin: 'subvenire' — to come to help
• 'State subvention', 'receive a subvention' — an official subsidy

quiz.question: The study of locomotion examined how different animals moved through their environments. What is locomotion?
options: The ability to move from place to place — the act of moving
The biological mechanism by which animals changed their position
The range of strategies different species used to navigate their habitats
The physical process underlying walking, swimming, and flight
quiz.answer: The ability to move from place to place — the act of moving
quiz.explanation:
Locomotion is self-propelled movement — the ability to move from place to place.
• locomotion = movement from place to place
• From Latin: 'locus' (place) + 'movere' (to move)
• 'Animal locomotion', 'steam locomotion' — self-powered movement

quiz.question: The caption beneath the photograph explained what it showed. What is a caption?
options: A title or brief text accompanying an image — an explanatory label
The identifying information printed below a photograph, as the evidence showed
A short text that gave context to a visual
The title placed beneath an image in a publication
quiz.answer: A title or brief text accompanying an image — an explanatory label
quiz.explanation:
A caption is a label — a brief text explaining or identifying an image.
• caption = a title or explanation accompanying an image
• From Latin: 'captio' — a seizure
• 'Photo caption', 'closed captions' — text accompanying a visual

quiz.question: The fraud was built entirely on deception — every claim had been false. What is deception?
options: The act of misleading — causing someone to believe something false
A systematic misrepresentation sustained over a long period, as the evidence showed
The foundation of a scheme built on false claims
Dishonesty serious enough to constitute a criminal offence
quiz.answer: The act of misleading — causing someone to believe something false
quiz.explanation:
Deception is deliberate misleading — causing a false belief.
• deception = the act of causing someone to believe a falsehood
• From Latin: 'decipere' — to catch
• 'Practice deception', 'self-deception' — deliberately misleading

quiz.question: She had been with the company since its inception twenty years ago. What is inception?
options: The beginning — the moment of establishment or founding
The period before the company had established its identity
The founding moment that shaped everything that followed
The starting point from which the organisation had grown
quiz.answer: The beginning — the moment of establishment or founding
quiz.explanation:
Inception is the beginning — the point at which something starts.
• inception = the beginning of something
• From Latin: 'incipere' — to begin
• 'Since inception', 'at the inception of the project' — from the start

quiz.question: The conception of the design was brilliant even if the execution fell short. What is conception?
options: The forming of an idea or plan — the initial creative act
The original vision that had preceded and shaped the final outcome
The stage at which creativity was highest
The starting point of a design process that produced mixed results
quiz.answer: The forming of an idea or plan — the initial creative act
quiz.explanation:
Conception is the forming of an idea — the creative or mental act of origination.
• conception = the forming of a concept; also the beginning of pregnancy
• From Latin: 'concipere' — to take in
• 'The original conception', 'a new conception' — forming an idea

quiz.question: Public perception of the company had shifted after the scandal. What is perception?
options: The way something is understood or interpreted — an impression formed
The public response to the events as measured by surveys and polling, as the evidence showed
The collective judgement that shaped how people related to the brand
The image that had been damaged and would take time to rebuild
quiz.answer: The way something is understood or interpreted — an impression formed
quiz.explanation:
Perception is the way something is seen — an impression or interpretation.
• perception = the way something is regarded or interpreted
• From Latin: 'percipere' — to seize
• 'Public perception', 'sensory perception' — how something is received

quiz.question: The doctor issued a prescription for the antibiotic. What is a prescription?
options: A written instruction from a doctor authorising medicine to be dispensed
The document that entitled the patient to collect medication from a pharmacy
The clinical decision that determined what treatment the patient received
The formal record of what medication the doctor had recommended
quiz.answer: A written instruction from a doctor authorising medicine to be dispensed
quiz.explanation:
A prescription is a doctor's written instruction — authorising the dispensing of medicine.
• prescription = a doctor's written instruction for medicine; a rule
• From Latin: 'praescribere' — to write beforehand
• 'Prescription medicine', 'on prescription' — medicine requiring a doctor's order

quiz.question: The law operates on a presumption of innocence. What is a presumption?
options: An assumption taken as true until proved otherwise
The default position from which a court begins its deliberations
A legal principle that determined how the burden of proof was allocated
The starting point required by fairness before evidence was weighed
quiz.answer: An assumption taken as true until proved otherwise
quiz.explanation:
A presumption is an assumption — something taken as true unless disproved.
• presumption = an assumption taken as true; audacious behaviour
• From Latin: 'praesumere' — to take for granted
• 'Presumption of innocence', 'the presumption is that' — assumed unless proved otherwise

quiz.question: The soldier faced court martial for desertion. What is desertion?
options: The act of abandoning a duty or post — especially military duty
A criminal offence under military law with severe penalties
An act of cowardice that his superior officers could not overlook
The departure from a position without authorisation or permission
quiz.answer: The act of abandoning a duty or post — especially military duty
quiz.explanation:
Desertion is abandonment of duty — especially the unauthorised leaving of military service.
• desertion = the act of deserting, especially a military post
• From Latin: 'deserere' — to abandon
• 'Charged with desertion', 'desertion of duty' — abandoning obligation

quiz.question: She learned self-assertion — the ability to state her needs without apology. What is self-assertion?
options: The confident expression of one's own rights or opinions
A skill that required overcoming the habit of deferring to others
The ability to communicate her needs clearly and without guilt
A quality developed through practice and supported by professional training
quiz.answer: The confident expression of one's own rights or opinions
quiz.explanation:
Self-assertion is confident expression of one's own interests — standing up for oneself.
• self-assertion = the confident expression of one's own opinions or rights
• From 'self' + 'assertion'
• 'Practise self-assertion', 'assertiveness training' — standing up for oneself

quiz.question: Digestion converts food into nutrients the body can absorb. What is digestion?
options: The process of breaking down food into absorbable substances
The biological mechanism that extracted energy from what was eaten
The series of chemical reactions that converted food to fuel
A bodily process that began in the mouth and ended in the intestine
quiz.answer: The process of breaking down food into absorbable substances
quiz.explanation:
Digestion is the breakdown of food — the process of extracting nutrients.
• digestion = the breaking down of food by the body
• From Latin: 'digerere' — to separate
• 'Digestive system', 'aid digestion' — the food-processing process

quiz.question: Ingestion of the chemical caused severe internal damage. What is ingestion?
options: The taking of food, drink, or another substance into the body
The act that introduced the chemical into the body
The point at which exposure became poisoning
The consumption of a substance that caused harm when absorbed, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: The taking of food, drink, or another substance into the body
quiz.explanation:
Ingestion is taking in — consuming food or substances by mouth.
• ingestion = the act of taking food or other substances into the body
• From Latin: 'ingerere' — to carry in
• 'Ingestion of toxins', 'accidental ingestion' — taking into the body by mouth

quiz.question: She sought retribution for the injustice done to her family. What is retribution?
options: Punishment inflicted in return for wrongdoing — revenge
The kind of justice that required someone to suffer for what they had done
A response that balanced the harm caused with an equivalent consequence
An act motivated by the desire to punish rather than to prevent future harm
quiz.answer: Punishment inflicted in return for wrongdoing — revenge
quiz.explanation:
Retribution is punishment in return — paying back harm with harm.
• retribution = punishment for wrongdoing; requital
• From Latin: 'retribuere' — to pay back
• 'Divine retribution', 'seek retribution' — punitive payback

quiz.question: The prosecution presented its case over three days. What is prosecution?
options: The legal process of pursuing charges against a defendant
The side in a trial that argued for the defendant's guilt
The party that presented evidence that an offence had been committed
The legal team responsible for establishing the case against the accused
quiz.answer: The legal process of pursuing charges against a defendant
quiz.explanation:
A prosecution is legal action — the process of pursuing criminal charges.
• prosecution = the institution or conduct of legal proceedings
• From Latin: 'prosequi' — to pursue
• 'Bring a prosecution', 'the prosecution rests' — pursuing legal charges

quiz.question: His locution was precise — every word chosen for its exact meaning. What is locution?
options: A style of speech — the manner and form of verbal expression
A command of language that reflected extensive reading
The verbal precision that set his communication apart from others
A quality of expression developed through years of careful attention to language
quiz.answer: A style of speech — the manner and form of verbal expression
quiz.explanation:
Locution is a style or form of speech — the way words are used in expression.
• locution = a particular style of speech; a phrase or expression
• From Latin: 'locutio' — a speaking
• 'A particular locution', 'formal locution' — manner of speaking

quiz.question: She took elocution lessons to improve her stage presence. What is elocution?
options: The skill of clear, effective public speaking — diction and delivery
The training that developed projection and clarity for performance, as the evidence showed
A discipline that combined vocal technique with rhetorical skill
The art of speaking well that was taught as a formal subject
quiz.answer: The skill of clear, effective public speaking — diction and delivery
quiz.explanation:
Elocution is the art of speaking clearly — especially in public performance.
• elocution = the skill of clear, expressive public speaking
• From Latin: 'eloqui' — to speak out
• 'Elocution lessons', 'elocution teacher' — the art of clear speech

quiz.question: His circumlocution on the key question frustrated those who wanted a direct answer. What is circumlocution?
options: The use of many words where few would do — avoiding directness
A verbal technique that said a great deal without committing to anything
A way of speaking that consumed time without advancing understanding
The art of speaking at length while refusing to address the point
quiz.answer: The use of many words where few would do — avoiding directness
quiz.explanation:
Circumlocution is using many words to avoid saying something directly.
• circumlocution = the use of more words than necessary to express a meaning
• From Latin: 'circum' (around) + 'locutio' (speech)
• 'Without circumlocution', 'resort to circumlocution' — talking around a point

quiz.question: She performed her morning ablutions before leaving the house. What are ablutions?
options: The act of washing oneself — a ritual or habitual cleansing
The morning routine of personal hygiene before beginning the day
The habits of cleanliness maintained as a matter of daily routine
The actions associated with preparing the body for the day ahead
quiz.answer: The act of washing oneself — a ritual or habitual cleansing
quiz.explanation:
Ablutions are acts of washing — especially the ritual cleansing of the body.
• ablution = the act of washing oneself; a religious cleansing rite
• From Latin: 'abluere' — to wash away
• 'Morning ablutions', 'perform ablutions' — washing and personal hygiene

quiz.question: There was a clear diminution in the quality of the work as the team shrank. What is diminution?
options: A reduction in size, extent, or quality — a lessening
A decline that became visible when the supporting resources were reduced
A measurable drop in quality linked to a loss of personnel
A deterioration that could be traced to specific organisational changes
quiz.answer: A reduction in size, extent, or quality — a lessening
quiz.explanation:
Diminution is a reduction — a decrease in size, quality, or intensity.
• diminution = a reduction; the act of diminishing
• From Latin: 'diminuere' — to lessen
• 'Diminution of rights', 'without diminution' — reduction or lessening

quiz.question: The war left millions in destitution — without shelter, food, or income. What is destitution?
options: Extreme poverty — the state of lacking basic necessities
The condition of those who had lost everything in the conflict
The most severe form of poverty that humanitarian organisations addressed
A level of deprivation that threatened survival without assistance
quiz.answer: Extreme poverty — the state of lacking basic necessities
quiz.explanation:
Destitution is extreme poverty — complete lack of means to survive.
• destitution = extreme poverty; the lack of basic necessities
• From Latin: 'destituere' — to abandon
• 'Fall into destitution', 'destitute refugees' — complete poverty

quiz.question: The court ordered restitution to the victims of the fraud. What is restitution?
options: The return or restoration of what was wrongly taken — compensation
The financial remedy that returned victims to their previous position
A judicial order requiring payment to those who had suffered loss
The form of justice that prioritised restoring the victim over punishing the offender
quiz.answer: The return or restoration of what was wrongly taken — compensation
quiz.explanation:
Restitution is restoration — returning or compensating for what was wrongly taken.
• restitution = the restoring of something lost or taken; compensation
• From Latin: 'restituere' — to restore
• 'Make restitution', 'restitution order' — restoring or compensating

quiz.question: Marriage is one of the oldest social institutions in human society. What is an institution?
options: An established organisation or practice — a structure with social significance
A practice so embedded in social life that it shapes expectations
A form of relationship recognised and regulated across cultures
A social arrangement whose continued existence was taken for granted, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: An established organisation or practice — a structure with social significance
quiz.explanation:
An institution is an established practice or organisation — with broad social function.
• institution = an established organisation or practice with social significance
• From Latin: 'instituere' — to set up
• 'Social institution', 'educational institution' — established practice or organisation

quiz.question: The constitution set out the rights and obligations of all citizens. What is a constitution?
options: The fundamental law or principles governing a state or organisation
The document that defined the relationship between citizens and the state
A set of principles that could only be changed through a special procedure
The legal foundation on which all other laws and institutions rested
quiz.answer: The fundamental law or principles governing a state or organisation
quiz.explanation:
A constitution is the fundamental law — the principles governing a state.
• constitution = the fundamental principles or laws of a state; also bodily condition
• From Latin: 'constituere' — to establish
• 'Written constitution', 'constitutional rights' — the foundational law

quiz.question: The recipe required two gallons of apple cider. What is a gallon?
options: A unit of liquid measure — equal to eight pints in the UK
A traditional volume used in both cooking and commercial trading
A unit of capacity whose precise value differed between countries
A measurement sufficient for a large batch of the recipe
quiz.answer: A unit of liquid measure — equal to eight pints in the UK
quiz.explanation:
A gallon is a unit of liquid volume — eight pints in the UK, about 3.8 litres in the US.
• gallon = a unit of liquid capacity
• From Old French: 'jalon'
• 'Gallons of milk', 'miles per gallon' — a liquid volume measurement

quiz.question: The cathedral's carillon rang out across the city at noon. What is a carillon?
options: A set of bells played from a keyboard — a musical bell tower
The bells housed in a church tower and rung in sequence
A musical instrument consisting of tuned bells struck by hammers
A tower instrument whose sound was audible across the surrounding area
quiz.answer: A set of bells played from a keyboard — a musical bell tower
quiz.explanation:
A carillon is a set of bells played musically — typically from a keyboard mechanism.
• carillon = a set of bells played as a musical instrument
• From Old French: 'quarregnon' — a set of four bells
• 'Play the carillon', 'carillon tower' — a set of musical bells

quiz.question: A colon introduces what follows — a list, a quotation, or an explanation. What is a colon?
options: The punctuation mark (:) used to introduce a list or explanation
A typographical mark that separated introductory material from what it announced
A punctuation device that drew attention to what was coming
The mark used to introduce direct speech or a block quotation
quiz.answer: The punctuation mark (:) used to introduce a list or explanation
quiz.explanation:
A colon is the punctuation mark (:) — introducing a list, explanation, or quotation.
• colon = the punctuation mark (:); also a part of the large intestine
• From Greek: 'kolon' — a limb
• 'Use a colon', 'colon in punctuation' — introducing what follows

quiz.question: The electricity pylons marched across the hillside in a straight line. What is a pylon?
options: A tall steel structure that supports overhead power lines
The metal framework used to carry high-voltage electricity cables
A structure whose height was determined by the voltage of the cables
A piece of infrastructure that crossed the landscape in grid-like patterns
quiz.answer: A tall steel structure that supports overhead power lines
quiz.explanation:
A pylon is a tall lattice tower — supporting overhead electrical cables.
• pylon = a tall structure for supporting electricity cables or marking boundaries
• From Greek: 'pylon' — a gateway
• 'Electricity pylon', 'runway pylon' — a tall supporting tower

quiz.question: Wild salmon migrate from the sea back to the rivers where they were born. What is remarkable about salmon?
options: Their ability to return to their birth river to spawn — an extraordinary feat
Their capacity to change colour as they prepare for migration
The energy they expend navigating their way back from the open sea, as the evidence showed
The accuracy of their navigation across hundreds of miles
quiz.answer: Their ability to return to their birth river to spawn — an extraordinary feat
quiz.explanation:
Salmon are remarkable for migrating back to their exact birthplace to spawn.
• salmon = a large fish that migrates from sea to river to breed
• From Latin: 'salmo'
• 'Salmon run', 'smoked salmon', 'salmon pink' — the migratory fish

quiz.question: The vicar delivered a sermon on the importance of forgiveness. What is a sermon?
options: A religious address — a speech on a moral or religious theme
The central teaching element of a Christian church service
A formal address designed to instruct a congregation
A moral or spiritual address delivered from a position of authority
quiz.answer: A religious address — a speech on a moral or religious theme
quiz.explanation:
A sermon is a religious speech — a moral or spiritual address in a church service.
• sermon = a religious or moral discourse delivered in a church
• From Latin: 'sermo' — a talk
• 'Preach a sermon', 'sermon on the mount' — a religious address

quiz.question: Shakespeare is firmly part of the literary canon. What is a canon?
options: The accepted body of important works — the established standard
The list of texts considered essential reading in a literary tradition
A set of works defined by consensus as central to a field
The collection of texts that shaped the tradition and defined its standards
quiz.answer: The accepted body of important works — the established standard
quiz.explanation:
A canon is the accepted body — the authoritative set of important works or rules.
• canon = the accepted body of important works; a rule or standard
• From Greek: 'kanon' — a rule
• 'Literary canon', 'musical canon', 'canon law' — the authoritative body

quiz.question: The extra bank holiday was a boon to the tourism industry. What is a boon?
options: A welcome benefit — something greatly appreciated
A positive development whose effects were felt across the sector
An advantage that arrived without anyone having asked for it
A change in circumstances that benefited a particular group
quiz.answer: A welcome benefit — something greatly appreciated
quiz.explanation:
A boon is a welcome benefit — something greatly valued.
• boon = a thing that is helpful or beneficial; a favour
• From Old Norse: 'bon' — a prayer
• 'A real boon', 'a boon to health' — a helpful and welcome benefit

quiz.question: She cocooned herself in routine after the bereavement, rarely leaving the house. What does 'cocooned' mean here?
options: Wrapped or enclosed in a protective shelter — insulated from the world
Retreating into familiar patterns as a response to grief
Using habit as a substitute for the engagement she was not ready for, as the evidence showed
Creating conditions that reduced the demands made on her
quiz.answer: Wrapped or enclosed in a protective shelter — insulated from the world
quiz.explanation:
To cocoon means to enclose in protection — insulating from the outside world.
• cocoon = the silky case of a caterpillar; to wrap protectively
• From French: 'cocon'
• 'Cocooned in warmth', 'cocoon of safety' — wrapped in protection

quiz.question: The media tycoon owned newspapers, television stations, and websites across three continents. What is a tycoon?
options: A very wealthy and powerful business person
A media proprietor whose holdings gave him disproportionate influence
An entrepreneur who had built an empire from a single original venture
A businessman whose wealth came from a combination of sectors
quiz.answer: A very wealthy and powerful business person
quiz.explanation:
A tycoon is a very wealthy and powerful business figure.
• tycoon = a wealthy and powerful person in business
• From Japanese: 'taikun' — great lord
• 'Media tycoon', 'oil tycoon' — a dominant business magnate

quiz.question: The village was built on the edge of a shallow lagoon. What is a lagoon?
options: A shallow body of water separated from the sea by a reef or sandbar
A coastal feature common in tropical and subtropical regions
A stretch of still water protected from the open sea
A shallow enclosed sea area behind a barrier of sand or coral, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A shallow body of water separated from the sea by a reef or sandbar
quiz.explanation:
A lagoon is a shallow coastal body of water — separated from the sea by a barrier.
• lagoon = a shallow body of water separated from the sea
• From Italian: 'laguna'
• 'Coral lagoon', 'Venice lagoon' — shallow water behind a barrier

quiz.question: The typhoon caused widespread destruction across the island. What is a typhoon?
options: A tropical cyclone occurring in the western Pacific — a hurricane
A severe storm that combined high winds with intense rainfall
A weather system capable of causing catastrophic damage in coastal areas
A rotating storm system that developed over warm ocean water
quiz.answer: A tropical cyclone occurring in the western Pacific — a hurricane
quiz.explanation:
A typhoon is a tropical cyclone — a severe storm in the western Pacific.
• typhoon = a tropical cyclone in the western Pacific
• From Chinese: 'tai fung' — great wind
• 'Typhoon season', 'hit by a typhoon' — a Pacific hurricane

quiz.question: The satirical magazine lampooned the minister with a caricature that perfectly captured his pomposity. What does 'lampoon' mean?
options: To ridicule publicly — to mock through satire
To produce a visual representation designed to embarrass its subject
To use exaggeration to expose the gap between manner and substance
To deploy satirical skill against a target whose pretensions invited it
quiz.answer: To ridicule publicly — to mock through satire
quiz.explanation:
To lampoon means to ridicule publicly — to satirise with sharp mockery.
• lampoon = a virulent piece of satire; to mock satirically
• From French: 'lampon' — a drinking song
• 'Lampoon a politician', 'political lampoon' — sharp satirical mockery

quiz.question: The whale was struck by a harpoon fired from the vessel. What is a harpoon?
options: A barbed spear used for hunting whales and large fish
A weapon fired with enough force to penetrate the thick skin of a whale
A device designed to both strike and hold a large marine animal
The tool of the whaling industry whose use was eventually restricted
quiz.answer: A barbed spear used for hunting whales and large fish
quiz.explanation:
A harpoon is a barbed spear — fired to strike and hold large sea creatures.
• harpoon = a barbed spear used in hunting whales and large fish
• From Dutch: 'harpoen'
• 'Fire a harpoon', 'harpoon gun' — the whaler's weapon

quiz.question: The sailors were marooned on the island after their ship sank. What does 'marooned' mean?
options: Stranded in an isolated place — left without means of escape
Abandoned in a location from which they could not be easily rescued
Cut off from civilisation through circumstances outside their control
Left in a position where survival depended on what they could find locally
quiz.answer: Stranded in an isolated place — left without means of escape
quiz.explanation:
Marooned means stranded — left alone in an isolated place with no way out.
• maroon = to strand in an isolated place; a dark brownish-red colour
• From Spanish: 'cimarrón' — wild
• 'Marooned on a desert island' — stranded without means of leaving

quiz.question: History has judged him a poltroon — he deserted his men at the moment of greatest danger. What is a poltroon?
options: A contemptible coward — someone who fails through lack of courage
A military figure whose reputation was destroyed by a single act
A leader who valued his own safety over his duty to those he commanded
A person whose cowardice was remembered long after his other qualities
quiz.answer: A contemptible coward — someone who fails through lack of courage
quiz.explanation:
A poltroon is a contemptible coward — a spiritless, despicable failure of nerve.
• poltroon = an utter coward
• From Italian: 'poltrone' — a lazy man
• 'A complete poltroon', 'poltroonery' — contemptible cowardice

quiz.question: The monsoon arrived two weeks late, devastating the harvest. What is a monsoon?
options: A seasonal wind system bringing heavy rainfall — especially in South Asia
The annual period of heavy rain on which the harvest depended
A meteorological system that transformed the landscape for several months
A weather pattern whose arrival or delay had major agricultural consequences
quiz.answer: A seasonal wind system bringing heavy rainfall — especially in South Asia
quiz.explanation:
The monsoon is a seasonal wind system — bringing heavy rainfall to South and Southeast Asia.
• monsoon = a seasonal wind bringing rain; the rainy season itself
• From Arabic: 'mawsim' — a season
• 'Monsoon season', 'monsoon rains' — the seasonal South Asian rainfall

quiz.question: The bassoon provided the deep bass foundation of the woodwind section. What is a bassoon?
options: A large, low-pitched woodwind instrument played with a double reed
The bass instrument of the woodwind family in an orchestra
A woodwind instrument whose range matched the double bass in strings
A large wooden instrument that produced the lowest tones in its section
quiz.answer: A large, low-pitched woodwind instrument played with a double reed
quiz.explanation:
A bassoon is a bass woodwind instrument — large, low-pitched, with a double reed.
• bassoon = a large bass woodwind instrument
• From Italian: 'bassone' — a large bass instrument
• 'Play the bassoon', 'bassoon concerto' — the bass woodwind

quiz.question: He was a media baron whose influence extended across the government of the day. What is a baron?
options: A person with great power in a particular field — originally a noble rank
A figure whose wealth gave him leverage over political decision-making, as the evidence showed
An entrepreneur whose holdings made him impossible to ignore
A proprietor whose control of media gave him unusual social power
quiz.answer: A person with great power in a particular field — originally a noble rank
quiz.explanation:
A baron is a powerful figure — originally a title of nobility, now also an industrial magnate.
• baron = a member of the lowest order of nobility; a powerful figure in industry
• From Old French: 'baron' — a warrior
• 'Oil baron', 'press baron' — a powerful figure in a field

quiz.question: The gridiron was packed with fans for the championship game. What is a gridiron?
options: An American football field — or the sport itself
The playing surface marked with parallel lines that gave it its name
The stadium in which the championship was decided
The sporting arena associated with the game
quiz.answer: An American football field — or the sport itself
quiz.explanation:
A gridiron is an American football field — named for its grid of parallel lines.
• gridiron = an American football field; the sport itself; also a cooking grate
• From 'grid' + 'iron'
• 'Gridiron football', 'on the gridiron' — American football

quiz.question: The factory had badly damaged its environs over decades of pollution. What are environs?
options: The surrounding area — the district or region around a place
The landscape and communities affected by the factory's activities
The natural and human environment that surrounded the site
The area whose water and air quality had been affected
quiz.answer: The surrounding area — the district or region around a place
quiz.explanation:
Environs are the surrounding areas — the district around a place.
• environs = the surroundings of a place
• From French: 'environner' — to surround
• 'London and its environs', 'in the environs of' — the surrounding district

quiz.question: A neutron is one of the particles found in the nucleus of an atom. What is a neutron?
options: A subatomic particle with no electric charge found in atomic nuclei
One of the two types of particle that made up most of an atom's mass
A particle that distinguished isotopes of the same element
A nuclear particle that participated in fission reactions
quiz.answer: A subatomic particle with no electric charge found in atomic nuclei
quiz.explanation:
A neutron is a neutral subatomic particle — found in the nucleus alongside protons.
• neutron = a subatomic particle with no electric charge
• From Latin: 'neuter' — neither
• 'Neutron star', 'neutron bomb' — the electrically neutral nuclear particle

quiz.question: He was charged with treason for passing state secrets to a foreign power. What is treason?
options: Betrayal of one's country — acting against the state
The most serious act of disloyalty available under criminal law
A crime defined by its target being the state rather than an individual
An offence that carried the most severe penalties in the legal system
quiz.answer: Betrayal of one's country — acting against the state
quiz.explanation:
Treason is betrayal of one's country — acting against the state or its ruler.
• treason = the crime of betraying one's country
• From Old French: 'traison' — a betrayal
• 'High treason', 'commit treason' — betrayal of the state

quiz.question: The mason cut and laid the stone blocks with extraordinary precision. What is a mason?
options: A craftsperson who works with stone or brick
A tradesperson whose skills were essential to large construction projects
A builder who specialised in working with stone materials
A craftsperson whose work was visible in the most durable buildings
quiz.answer: A craftsperson who works with stone or brick
quiz.explanation:
A mason is a craftsperson who builds with stone — cutting and laying masonry.
• mason = a person who builds with stone or brick
• From Old French: 'maçon'
• 'Stone mason', 'Freemasonry' — working with stone

quiz.question: She acted as liaison between the two departments, coordinating their work. What is a liaison?
options: A person or communication that links different groups — a connecting link
The coordination function that prevented the two departments from duplicating work
A role that required communication skills as well as technical knowledge
A position created because the two departments were not communicating
quiz.answer: A person or communication that links different groups — a connecting link
quiz.explanation:
A liaison is a connection — a person or channel linking different groups.
• liaison = communication between different groups; a person who facilitates this
• From French: 'lier' — to bind
• 'Liaison officer', 'act as liaison' — linking groups

quiz.question: The priest offered a benison over the gathering before it dispersed. What is a benison?
options: A blessing — a spoken expression of divine favour
A formal religious act marking the close of a gathering
A prayer invoking God's protection over those present
A traditional religious form used at the end of a ceremony
quiz.answer: A blessing — a spoken expression of divine favour
quiz.explanation:
A benison is a blessing — an archaic word for benediction.
• benison = a blessing
• From Old French: 'beneiçon'
• 'A priestly benison', poetic for 'benediction' — a spoken blessing

quiz.question: By comparison with its rivals, the company performed impressively. What is a comparison?
options: An examination of similarities and differences — a placing side by side
A relative assessment that used another company as the benchmark
An evaluation that established a standard against which to measure, as the evidence showed
The analytical exercise that made the performance meaningful
quiz.answer: An examination of similarities and differences — a placing side by side
quiz.explanation:
A comparison is an examination of similarities and differences — placing side by side.
• comparison = the act of comparing; an examination of similarities
• From Latin: 'comparare' — to match
• 'By comparison', 'comparison shopping', 'beyond comparison' — examining together

quiz.question: The company jettisoned its loss-making divisions to focus on its core business. What does 'jettison' mean?
options: To throw away or discard — to abandon what is unnecessary
To make a strategic decision to end activities that were not profitable
To remove parts of a business that had become a drag on performance
To accept short-term losses to improve long-term prospects
quiz.answer: To throw away or discard — to abandon what is unnecessary
quiz.explanation:
To jettison means to throw overboard — to discard what is no longer needed.
• jettison = to throw away or abandon; originally to throw cargo overboard
• From Old French: 'jeter' — to throw
• 'Jettison a plan', 'jettison cargo' — discarding what is unwanted

quiz.question: The fire was not accidental — it had been set deliberately and was treated as arson. What is arson?
options: The criminal act of deliberately setting fire to property
A serious criminal offence where fire was used as a weapon
An intentional act distinguishing a crime from an accident
The wilful destruction of property through fire
quiz.answer: The criminal act of deliberately setting fire to property
quiz.explanation:
Arson is deliberately starting a fire — a serious criminal offence.
• arson = the criminal act of deliberately setting fire to property
• From Latin: 'ardere' — to burn
• 'Commit arson', 'arson attack' — deliberate setting of fire

quiz.question: The conductor raised the baton and the orchestra fell silent. What is a baton?
options: A thin stick used by a conductor to direct an orchestra
The implement whose movement communicated tempo and dynamics
A small wand used to signal tempo and expression to musicians
The conductor's tool whose movements were followed by the ensemble
quiz.answer: A thin stick used by a conductor to direct an orchestra
quiz.explanation:
A baton is a thin stick — used by a conductor to direct an orchestra.
• baton = a thin stick used by a conductor; also a relay baton or police truncheon
• From French: 'bâton' — a stick
• 'Conductor's baton', 'relay baton' — a stick for directing or passing

quiz.question: He was no simpleton — the naive act was a deliberate performance. What is a simpleton?
options: A person regarded as foolish or lacking common sense
Someone whose apparent simplicity concealed more than it revealed
A person whose behaviour suggested limited intellectual ability
Someone too easily deceived to navigate complex situations
quiz.answer: A person regarded as foolish or lacking common sense
quiz.explanation:
A simpleton is a foolish person — one regarded as lacking common sense.
• simpleton = a person lacking in common sense
• From 'simple' + '-ton'
• 'A complete simpleton', 'no simpleton' — a foolish person

quiz.question: Plankton forms the base of the ocean food chain. What is plankton?
options: Microscopic organisms that drift in water — the foundation of aquatic food chains
The tiny organisms on which all larger marine life ultimately depended
A collective term for the floating life that filled the upper ocean layers
The organisms whose population health indicated the state of marine ecosystems, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Microscopic organisms that drift in water — the foundation of aquatic food chains
quiz.explanation:
Plankton are microscopic drifting organisms — plants and animals in water.
• plankton = small organisms drifting in water; the base of aquatic food chains
• From Greek: 'planktos' — wandering
• 'Phytoplankton', 'zooplankton', 'plankton bloom' — drifting microorganisms

quiz.question: The wanton destruction of the historic building provoked public outrage. What does 'wanton' mean?
options: Deliberate and unprovoked — done without justification or restraint
More destructive than could be explained by any legitimate purpose, as the evidence showed
So unnecessary as to constitute a kind of contempt for the public
Carried out without regard for what was being lost
quiz.answer: Deliberate and unprovoked — done without justification or restraint
quiz.explanation:
Wanton means deliberate and without restraint — gratuitously harmful.
• wanton = deliberate and unprovoked; also: playfully mischievous
• From Middle English: 'wantowen' — undisciplined
• 'Wanton cruelty', 'wanton destruction' — gratuitous, unjustified harm

quiz.question: The traditional recipe called for mutton rather than lamb. What is mutton?
options: The meat of a fully grown sheep — distinct from younger lamb
A cut of meat with a stronger flavour than the younger equivalent
The sheep-derived ingredient used in traditional slow-cooked dishes
A meat prized for its richness but requiring longer cooking
quiz.answer: The meat of a fully grown sheep — distinct from younger lamb
quiz.explanation:
Mutton is the meat of a fully grown sheep — stronger-flavoured than lamb.
• mutton = the flesh of a fully grown sheep used as food
• From Old French: 'moton' — a sheep
• 'Mutton chop', 'mutton dressed as lamb' — sheep meat

quiz.question: The children drew pictures with wax crayons. What is a crayon?
options: A stick of coloured wax used for drawing
A drawing implement made from pigment and wax for children
A colouring tool that worked on most paper surfaces
A drawing medium whose marks could not be erased cleanly
quiz.answer: A stick of coloured wax used for drawing
quiz.explanation:
A crayon is a coloured drawing stick — usually wax-based.
• crayon = a stick of coloured wax or chalk for drawing
• From French: 'crayon' — a pencil
• 'Wax crayon', 'colour with crayons' — a drawing stick

quiz.question: He looked back on his university years as a halcyon time. What does 'halcyon' mean?
options: Pleasantly peaceful — idyllically happy and calm
Remembered as better than it actually was
Free from the responsibilities that came with adult life
Defined by the friendships that had outlasted the period itself
quiz.answer: Pleasantly peaceful — idyllically happy and calm
quiz.explanation:
Halcyon means pleasantly peaceful — denoting a time of happiness and tranquillity.
• halcyon = denoting a period of peace and happiness
• From Greek: 'halcyon' — a kingfisher (fabled to breed in a calm sea)
• 'Halcyon days', 'halcyon youth' — a golden, peaceful time

quiz.question: The herald blazoned the coat of arms before the assembled knights. What does 'blazon' mean?
options: To describe or depict prominently — to display or proclaim
To read aloud the formal description of a heraldic device
To present the symbols of a lineage before a formal gathering
To announce with ceremony what each element of a coat of arms signified
quiz.answer: To describe or depict prominently — to display or proclaim
quiz.explanation:
To blazon means to proclaim or display prominently — or to describe heraldry.
• blazon = to describe heraldic arms; to proclaim or display prominently
• From Old French: 'blazon' — a shield
• 'Blazon a coat of arms', 'blazoned across the headlines' — displaying prominently

quiz.question: The harvest was stored in the barn until it could be sold. What is a barn?
options: A large farm building for storing grain, animals, or equipment
The central storage facility of a working agricultural enterprise
A structure large enough to hold the annual output of a farm
A building whose size reflected the scale of the farming operation
quiz.answer: A large farm building for storing grain, animals, or equipment
quiz.explanation:
A barn is a large farm building — for storing crops, housing animals, or keeping equipment.
• barn = a large farm building used for storage or housing livestock
• From Old English: 'bern' — barley house
• 'Hay barn', 'barn dance' — the central farm storage building

quiz.question: He spun a yarn about being delayed by wild weather — no one believed a word. What does 'spin a yarn' mean?
options: To tell a long, implausible story — especially to make excuses
To fabricate an explanation that had the form but not the content of truth
To construct a narrative whose purpose was to avoid responsibility
To deploy narrative skill in the service of an unlikely excuse
quiz.answer: To tell a long, implausible story — especially to make excuses
quiz.explanation:
To spin a yarn means to tell an improbable story — originally sailors' tales.
• yarn = a long improbable story; also: spun fibre for weaving
• From Old English: 'gearn' — spun fibre
• 'Spin a yarn', 'tell a yarn' — an implausible or adventurous story

quiz.question: She could discern a faint pattern in the noise that others had missed. What does 'discern' mean?
options: To perceive or distinguish — to recognise or make out
To find what was present but not obvious to ordinary observation
To apply the kind of attention that made hidden things visible
To see what was there by paying more careful attention than others had
quiz.answer: To perceive or distinguish — to recognise or make out
quiz.explanation:
To discern means to perceive — to recognise or distinguish what is not obvious.
• discern = to perceive or recognise clearly
• From Latin: 'discernere' — to separate
• 'Discern a pattern', 'discerning eye' — perceiving clearly

quiz.question: Ferns thrive in the damp shade of woodland floors. What is a fern?
options: A flowerless plant with feathery fronds that reproduces by spores
A shade-tolerant plant common in woodland and hedgerow habitats
A plant that lacks flowers but produces elaborate leafy fronds
A primitive plant found in environments with high moisture and shade
quiz.answer: A flowerless plant with feathery fronds that reproduces by spores
quiz.explanation:
A fern is a flowerless vascular plant — with feathery fronds, reproducing by spores.
• fern = a flowerless vascular plant with feathery fronds
• From Old English: 'fearn'
• 'Woodland fern', 'tree fern' — the spore-producing plant

quiz.question: The speaker gripped the lectern tightly as she addressed the packed hall. What is a lectern?
options: A tall stand with a slanted top from which a speaker addresses an audience
The furniture at the front of a hall from which notes were read
A reading stand whose height was adjustable for different speakers
The traditional furniture of public oratory placed at the front of a stage, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A tall stand with a slanted top from which a speaker addresses an audience
quiz.explanation:
A lectern is a reading stand — from which a speaker delivers an address.
• lectern = a tall stand with a slanting reading surface
• From Latin: 'legere' — to read
• 'Stand at the lectern', 'podium and lectern' — the speaker's reading stand

quiz.question: The judge's stern gaze made the defendant look away. What does 'stern' mean?
options: Serious and uncompromising — strict and severe
Communicating displeasure in a way that did not require words
More intense than the occasion would normally have called for
Appropriate to a person whose authority required visible seriousness
quiz.answer: Serious and uncompromising — strict and severe
quiz.explanation:
Stern means serious and strict — uncompromising and demanding.
• stern = serious and uncompromising; also: the rear of a ship
• From Old English: 'styrne' — severe
• 'Stern warning', 'stern face' — severe and uncompromising

quiz.question: The cistern collected rainwater from the roof for use in the dry season. What is a cistern?
options: A tank or reservoir for storing water
A container used to collect and hold rainwater
A water storage system that reduced dependence on the mains
An underground or roof-mounted container for water collection
quiz.answer: A tank or reservoir for storing water
quiz.explanation:
A cistern is a water tank — storing collected or piped water.
• cistern = a tank for storing water
• From Latin: 'cisterna' — an underground tank
• 'Toilet cistern', 'rainwater cistern' — a water storage tank

quiz.question: The cave system opened into a vast cavern large enough to hold a cathedral. What is a cavern?
options: A large underground chamber — a spacious cave
A natural hollow in rock large enough to be impressive in scale
The kind of geological feature formed over thousands of years
A space formed by the slow dissolution of limestone by water
quiz.answer: A large underground chamber — a spacious cave
quiz.explanation:
A cavern is a large cave — a spacious underground chamber.
• cavern = a large underground cave
• From Latin: 'cavus' — hollow
• 'Dark cavern', 'cavern system' — a large underground hollow

quiz.question: She had an inborn sense of rhythm that no amount of teaching could have given. What does 'inborn' mean?
options: Present from birth — existing naturally rather than acquired
Too fundamental to be attributed to a specific learning experience
A talent that expressed itself before formal training began
A quality that set her apart from those who had learned the same skills
quiz.answer: Present from birth — existing naturally rather than acquired
quiz.explanation:
Inborn means present from birth — natural rather than learned.
• inborn = natural; existing from birth
• From 'in-' + 'born'
• 'Inborn ability', 'inborn talent' — natural, not acquired

quiz.question: The witness had been suborned — paid to give false testimony. What does 'suborn' mean?
options: To bribe or induce someone to commit a wrongful act — especially perjury
To pay someone to change their testimony in a way that benefited one side
To corrupt a witness whose truthful evidence would have been damaging
To interfere with the course of justice through financial inducement
quiz.answer: To bribe or induce someone to commit a wrongful act — especially perjury
quiz.explanation:
To suborn means to bribe into wrongdoing — especially inducing perjury.
• suborn = to bribe or persuade someone to commit a crime, especially perjury
• From Latin: 'subornare' — to equip secretly
• 'Suborn a witness', 'subornation of perjury' — inducing false testimony

quiz.question: From a small acorn the mighty oak grows — an acorn is the seed of an oak tree. What is an acorn?
options: The nut or seed of an oak tree — a smooth oval fruit in a cup
The fruit produced by the oak that also served as food for wildlife
The seed from which a long-lived tree grew over many centuries
The small nut that illustrated how great things begin from small ones
quiz.answer: The nut or seed of an oak tree — a smooth oval fruit in a cup
quiz.explanation:
An acorn is an oak's nut — the small oval fruit in a woody cup.
• acorn = the nut of an oak tree
• From Old English: 'æcern' — a fruit or berry
• 'Acorn cup', 'oak acorn' — the fruit of the oak

quiz.question: The startup had reached unicorn status — a valuation of over a billion dollars. What is a unicorn?
options: Something rare and exceptional — originally a mythical horse with a horn
A startup valued at over one billion dollars before going public
A rare achievement in the startup world that attracted great attention, as the evidence showed
A company whose valuation had made it one of the most discussed
quiz.answer: Something rare and exceptional — originally a mythical horse with a horn
quiz.explanation:
A unicorn is something rare — originally a mythical beast; now also a billion-dollar startup.
• unicorn = a mythical horse with a horn; a very rare thing; a billion-dollar startup
• From Latin: 'unicornis' — one-horned
• 'Unicorn startup', 'rare as a unicorn' — extremely rare

quiz.question: She sounded the horn to warn the cyclists before overtaking. What is a horn?
options: A device that produces a loud warning sound
A safety feature required to be functioning on all road vehicles
The instrument used to signal the driver's intention to pass
A noise-making device whose use was governed by road traffic law
quiz.answer: A device that produces a loud warning sound
quiz.explanation:
A horn is a sound-producing device — used as a warning signal.
• horn = a device making a loud warning sound; also an animal's horn
• From Old English: 'horn'
• 'Sound the horn', 'horn of plenty', 'French horn' — a warning device or musical instrument

quiz.question: The experienced climbers had little patience for the greenhorn who underestimated the mountain. What is a greenhorn?
options: A person who is inexperienced and naive — a newcomer
Someone whose lack of experience made them a liability in a dangerous situation
A beginner whose confidence exceeded their actual ability
A newcomer whose assumptions about a situation proved incorrect
quiz.answer: A person who is inexperienced and naive — a newcomer
quiz.explanation:
A greenhorn is a naive newcomer — inexperienced and unfamiliar with the situation.
• greenhorn = an inexperienced and naive person
• From 'green' (immature) + 'horn' (a young animal's first horn)
• 'A real greenhorn', 'greenhorn mistake' — a naive newcomer

quiz.question: His timeworn excuses no longer fooled anyone. What does 'timeworn' mean?
options: Old and worn out through long use — hackneyed
Too familiar to produce the effect that had once made them effective
Repeated so often they had lost the credibility they never really had
Too well-known to those who had heard them before to be believed
quiz.answer: Old and worn out through long use — hackneyed
quiz.explanation:
Timeworn means worn out by time — old, trite, and worn through overuse.
• timeworn = worn or impaired by long use; hackneyed
• From 'time' + 'worn'
• 'Timeworn phrase', 'timeworn argument' — old and worn out

quiz.question: His careworn face told the story of the years since they had last met. What does 'careworn' mean?
options: Worn down by worry and anxiety — showing the marks of stress
Changed by the difficulties that the intervening years had brought
Marked by experience in a way that could not be concealed
Aged beyond his years by the burdens he had been carrying
quiz.answer: Worn down by worry and anxiety — showing the marks of stress
quiz.explanation:
Careworn means showing signs of anxiety — worn by worry and responsibility.
• careworn = showing the effects of prolonged worry
• From 'care' + 'worn'
• 'A careworn face', 'careworn expression' — marked by worry

quiz.question: The analogy was shopworn — everyone in the room had heard it before. What does 'shopworn' mean?
options: Faded and stale from overuse — having lost freshness
Familiar to the point where it no longer produced the intended effect
Too often repeated to have retained its original force
Borrowed so frequently it had ceased to be effective communication
quiz.answer: Faded and stale from overuse — having lost freshness
quiz.explanation:
Shopworn means trite and stale — worn out from overuse.
• shopworn = impaired by display in a shop; trite from overuse
• From 'shop' + 'worn'
• 'A shopworn phrase', 'shopworn idea' — lacking freshness from too much use

quiz.question: The judge adjourned the hearing until the following Monday. What does 'adjourn' mean?
options: To postpone or suspend a meeting — to break off for a later time
To pause proceedings with the intention of resuming later
To defer what remained unfinished to a specified future date, as the evidence showed
To close the current session while scheduling a continuation
quiz.answer: To postpone or suspend a meeting — to break off for a later time
quiz.explanation:
To adjourn means to suspend — to break off proceedings until a later time.
• adjourn = to break off proceedings until a later time
• From Old French: 'ajourner' — to appoint a day
• 'Adjourn a meeting', 'court adjourned' — suspending until later

quiz.question: The nation mourned the loss of a leader who had served for three decades. What does 'mourn' mean?
options: To feel or express grief — to show sorrow for a loss
To respond to a death with the emotions and rituals of grief
To observe the period of sadness that followed an important death
To express collectively what each person felt individually
quiz.answer: To feel or express grief — to show sorrow for a loss
quiz.explanation:
To mourn means to grieve — to feel and express sorrow at a loss.
• mourn = to feel grief; to express sorrow
• From Old English: 'murnan' — to be anxious
• 'Mourn a death', 'in mourning' — expressing grief

quiz.question: She spurned his repeated attempts to apologise. What does 'spurn' mean?
options: To reject with contempt — to refuse scornfully
To make clear that his apologies were neither wanted nor sufficient
To set aside an offer whose sincerity she had reason to doubt
To refuse in a way that communicated both her decision and her feelings
quiz.answer: To reject with contempt — to refuse scornfully
quiz.explanation:
To spurn means to reject contemptuously — to refuse with scorn.
• spurn = to reject with contempt
• From Old English: 'spornan' — to kick
• 'Spurn an offer', 'spurned lover' — contemptuous rejection

quiz.question: The community shunned him after the revelation of what he had done. What does 'shun' mean?
options: To persistently avoid — to keep away from
To withdraw all social contact as a collective act of disapproval
To make clear through absence that his presence was not welcome
To communicate rejection through the withdrawal of normal social interaction
quiz.answer: To persistently avoid — to keep away from
quiz.explanation:
To shun means to deliberately avoid — to persistently keep away from.
• shun = to persistently avoid or keep away from
• From Old English: 'scunian' — to abhor
• 'Shun publicity', 'shunned by the community' — deliberate avoidance

quiz.question: His speech was peppered with puns that divided the audience between groans and laughter. What is a pun?
options: A play on words — exploiting similar sounds to comic effect
A form of humour based on the multiple meanings of a single word
A joke that worked through the ambiguity of language
A verbal joke whose success depended on the audience noticing the double meaning
quiz.answer: A play on words — exploiting similar sounds to comic effect
quiz.explanation:
A pun is a wordplay — exploiting similar sounds or multiple meanings for comic effect.
• pun = the humorous use of words with similar sounds or multiple meanings
• Origin uncertain
• 'Make a pun', 'groan at a pun' — wordplay for comic effect

quiz.question: The success of the original film spawned three sequels and a television series. What does 'spawn' mean?
options: To give rise to — to produce or generate as a consequence
To create conditions from which further works could emerge
To become the foundation on which an entire franchise was built
To produce offspring both in number and in commercial scale
quiz.answer: To give rise to — to produce or generate as a consequence
quiz.explanation:
To spawn means to produce or generate — as a parent generates offspring.
• spawn = to generate; also the eggs of fish
• From Old French: 'espandre' — to pour out
• 'Spawn imitators', 'spawned a genre' — generating as a consequence

quiz.question: What began as a minor dispute became a full-blown diplomatic crisis. What does 'full-blown' mean?
options: Fully developed — having reached its most extreme or complete form
Too serious to be managed through the normal diplomatic channels
Developed beyond the point at which containment was possible
Escalated to a level that required intervention at the highest level
quiz.answer: Fully developed — having reached its most extreme or complete form
quiz.explanation:
Full-blown means fully developed — reaching its most complete or extreme state.
• full-blown = fully developed; at full maturity
• From 'full' + 'blown' (in full flower)
• 'Full-blown crisis', 'full-blown disease' — at the most developed stage

quiz.question: His colleagues felt he was clowning around when the situation demanded seriousness. What does 'clowning' mean?
options: Behaving foolishly — making jokes when the situation calls for gravity
Prioritising entertainment over the demands of the situation
Using humour as a way of avoiding engagement with a serious matter
Acting in a way that reduced the confidence of those who needed leadership
quiz.answer: Behaving foolishly — making jokes when the situation calls for gravity
quiz.explanation:
To clown means to behave foolishly — to play the clown when seriousness is needed.
• clown = a performer who entertains with comedy; to behave foolishly
• From Scandinavian languages; related to 'clod'
• 'Stop clowning around', 'circus clown' — foolish behaviour or comedy performer

quiz.question: The win was the crown of a career that had lasted thirty years. What does 'crown' mean here?
options: The highest achievement — the culminating glory of an endeavour
The final recognition of a career defined by sustained excellence
A success that retrospectively validated all that had preceded it
An achievement that no subsequent event could equal or surpass
quiz.answer: The highest achievement — the culminating glory of an endeavour
quiz.explanation:
A crown (figuratively) is the highest achievement — the glory that caps everything.
• crown = a royal headdress; also the highest achievement or culmination
• From Latin: 'corona' — a wreath
• 'The crown of his career', 'crown jewels' — highest honour or achievement

quiz.question: The ceiling was decorated with a fresco painted by an unknown Renaissance artist. What is a fresco?
options: A painting made on wet plaster — a wall or ceiling painting
A mural technique that fused pigment with the wall's surface
A form of decorative art characteristic of Italian Renaissance churches
A painting whose durability came from being part of the plaster
quiz.answer: A painting made on wet plaster — a wall or ceiling painting
quiz.explanation:
A fresco is a painting on fresh wet plaster — becoming part of the wall surface.
• fresco = a type of mural painted on wet plaster
• From Italian: 'fresco' — fresh
• 'Sistine Chapel fresco', 'paint in fresco' — painting on wet plaster

quiz.question: The tornado touched down at dusk and destroyed everything in a mile-wide path. What is a tornado?
options: A violently rotating column of air extending from a storm cloud to the ground
A weather event capable of producing wind speeds of several hundred miles an hour
A destructive meteorological phenomenon common in the American interior
A spinning column of air that caused destruction along a narrow path
quiz.answer: A violently rotating column of air extending from a storm cloud to the ground
quiz.explanation:
A tornado is a violent rotating column of air — a twister connecting cloud to ground.
• tornado = a violent rotating column of air
• From Spanish: 'tronada' — a thunderstorm
• 'Tornado warning', 'tornado alley' — a violent wind funnel

quiz.question: The desperado held up three banks before he was finally caught. What is a desperado?
options: A reckless, violent criminal — one who acts with desperate disregard for consequences
A criminal whose pattern of behaviour made eventual capture inevitable, as the evidence showed
An outlaw whose reputation for violence preceded him
A fugitive who had committed enough crimes to be widely sought
quiz.answer: A reckless, violent criminal — one who acts with desperate disregard for consequences
quiz.explanation:
A desperado is a reckless criminal — one driven by desperation to violence.
• desperado = a reckless criminal, especially in the old west
• From Spanish: 'desesperado' — desperate
• 'A desperado on the run', 'outlaw desperado' — a violent reckless criminal

quiz.question: His bravado concealed a deep anxiety about whether he could actually do the job. What is bravado?
options: A show of boldness that conceals fear — false bravery
A performance of confidence that substituted for the real thing
The outward presentation of certainty in the absence of inner certainty
A social strategy that prevented others from detecting his vulnerability
quiz.answer: A show of boldness that conceals fear — false bravery
quiz.explanation:
Bravado is a false show of boldness — displaying courage one does not truly feel.
• bravado = a show of boldness intended to impress
• From Spanish: 'bravada' — bragging
• 'All bravado', 'show of bravado' — performed boldness concealing fear

quiz.question: The government's plans were torpedoed by an unexpected ruling from the courts. What does 'torpedo' mean?
options: To destroy or ruin completely — to sink decisively
To produce an outcome that made the plans impossible to proceed with
To intervene in a way that removed the basis for the proposed action
To end a project through a single decisive judicial act
quiz.answer: To destroy or ruin completely — to sink decisively
quiz.explanation:
To torpedo means to destroy decisively — like an underwater weapon sinking a ship.
• torpedo = an underwater missile; to destroy or ruin decisively
• From Latin: 'torpedo' — the electric ray (a fish)
• 'Torpedo a deal', 'naval torpedo' — decisive destruction

quiz.question: His professional credo was simple: say what you mean and do what you say. What is a credo?
options: A statement of core beliefs — a personal creed
A principle that guided his professional conduct across all situations
A commitment simple enough to remember and important enough to keep
The value system that colleagues came to recognise as his defining characteristic
quiz.answer: A statement of core beliefs — a personal creed
quiz.explanation:
A credo is a personal creed — a statement of core beliefs and principles.
• credo = a statement of beliefs; a creed
• From Latin: 'credo' — I believe
• 'Company credo', 'personal credo' — a statement of beliefs

quiz.question: He wore a tuxedo to the awards ceremony. What is a tuxedo?
options: A formal black dinner jacket — semiformal evening wear
The outfit required at events with a black-tie dress code
A formal suit with a black jacket and matching trousers
The standard male formal dress for evening occasions
quiz.answer: A formal black dinner jacket — semiformal evening wear
quiz.explanation:
A tuxedo is a dinner jacket — formal black evening wear.
• tuxedo = a dinner jacket; formal evening wear
• Named after Tuxedo Park, New York
• 'Wear a tuxedo', 'black tuxedo' — formal evening wear

quiz.question: The music faded to a diminuendo before the final silence. What is a diminuendo?
options: A gradual decrease in volume — getting quieter
The musical direction indicating that sound should reduce in intensity
A passage where the dynamic level fell toward a concluding silence
The opposite of crescendo in a musical score
quiz.answer: A gradual decrease in volume — getting quieter
quiz.explanation:
A diminuendo is a gradual decrease in volume — getting progressively quieter.
• diminuendo = a gradual decrease in loudness
• From Italian: 'diminuire' — to diminish
• Opposite of crescendo; 'play diminuendo' — gradually quieter

quiz.question: The article was full of innuendo — nothing was stated directly but much was implied. What is innuendo?
options: An indirect suggestion — implying something without saying it openly
A form of communication that achieved its purpose through implication
Language whose real meaning required the reader to fill in the gap
A technique that conveyed what could not safely be stated
quiz.answer: An indirect suggestion — implying something without saying it openly
quiz.explanation:
An innuendo is an indirect suggestion — implying something without stating it.
• innuendo = an indirect or subtle reference, especially one that implies something negative
• From Latin: 'innuere' — to nod toward
• 'Subtle innuendo', 'full of innuendo' — suggesting without stating
